summary: prisma-callback impersonates Prisma and executes an undeclared native Go binary via preinstall
details: The package is published on npm as `prisma-callback` but its README, `packages/` tree, workflows, and LICENSE are a wholesale clone of the real prisma/prisma repository — the tarball advertises itself as Prisma to inflate trust and install counts. The only meaningful additions over upstream are two architecture-specific Go ELF binaries at the tarball root (`prisma-amd64` ~1.6 MB, `prisma-arm64` ~2.1 MB) and a `preinstall` script at `scripts/only-allow-pnpm.js`. On every `npm install`, the preinstall hook selects a binary based on `os.arch()` and executes it with `execSync(`${binary} lint`, { stdio: 'inherit' })`, running attacker-supplied opaque native code with the installer's privileges. There is no hash or signature verification, no download from a publisher-owned CDN, no declared build system that would produce these binaries from shipped source, and Prisma's real CLI does not ship or need a custom Go `lint` binary. A misleading comment (`This script is safe — it's only used for testing purposes.`) is placed at the top of the preinstall to lower reviewer suspicion. The combination of namespace impersonation of a top-tier npm package and install-time execution of an opaque native binary is an unambiguous supply-chain attack pattern.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
